food_subgroup: Sweet glutinous rice congee with tablea (Philippine ground cacao), condensed milk, and evaporated milk

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for champorado. Closest parent concept: sweet rice porridge with chocolate flavouring. No exact FoodOn term available as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for champorado. Closest parent category: sweet rice-based porridge/congee. Code A001001001001 is for 'Porridge, rice, sweet'.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for champorado or Filipino chocolate rice porridge as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Filipino bowl of champorado is approximately 300g, comprising roughly 180g glutinous rice congee base, 50g melted tablea, and 70g combined condensed and evaporated milk, yielding approximately 504 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS South and East Asian Food Composition Table (Philippines entries) supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for tablea/cacao and condensed milk components
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS South and East Asian Food Composition Table; USDA Agricultural Research Service SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Champorado nutrient values derived from FAO/INFOODS SEA Philippines food composition data for glutinous rice congee combined with USDA SR Legacy values for tablea/cacao (USDA FDC #19165 cocoa powder, unsweetened as closest analogue) and condensed milk (USDA FDC #01077). Per-100g values represent the composite dish at standard preparation ratios. FAO/INFOODS SEA is an approved primary regional source for Southeast Asian dishes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of glutinous rice and chocolate-based congee — analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (glutinous rice GI ~98; fat and protein from tablea and condensed milk moderate composite GI to ~69)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; peer-reviewed GI literature for glutinous rice and cocoa-fat moderation
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for champorado in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at ~69 using the following analogous evidence: (1) glutinous rice congee GI ~87–98 per University of Sydney GI Database entries for sticky/glutinous rice; (2) fat content from tablea (~4.5g fat/100g composite) and condensed milk significantly moderates the glycemic response, consistent with published fat-carbohydrate interaction data; (3) composite GI ~69 is a defensible medium-GI estimate. GL per 300g serving = (69 × 87g carbohydrate) / 100 = ~60, classified as very high GL. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~45mg/100g; ~135mg per 300g bowl without tuyo garnish)
hypertension.guidance: Champorado without tuyo is low in sodium (~135mg per bowl) and is generally suitable for hypertension management; however, adding tuyo (salted dried fish) as a traditional garnish raises sodium to approximately 935mg per serving, which would be a high-concern addition for hypertensive individuals. Theobromine from tablea cacao has mild vasodilatory properties and cocoa flavanols (~290mg per bowl) may modestly improve endothelial function with regular consumption.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic load (~GL 60 per 300g bowl) driven by glutinous rice base and condensed milk sugar content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Champorado carries a very high glycemic load of approximately 60 per standard 300g bowl — equivalent to the glucose impact of approximately 4 slices of white bread — making it a high-risk food for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; portion reduction to 100–150g and substitution of condensed milk with unsweetened evaporated milk are strongly recommended to reduce postprandial glucose excursion. Cocoa flavanols from tablea have modest insulin-sensitising effects but cannot offset the substantial carbohydrate load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~2.8g/100g; ~8.4g per bowl) from condensed milk and tablea, partially offset by cocoa flavanol cardioprotective effects
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each 300g bowl of champorado provides approximately 8.4g saturated fat, which represents a moderate cardiovascular concern; however, tablea cacao contributes approximately 290mg cocoa flavanols per bowl, which have demonstrated cardioprotective effects including improved endothelial function, reduced LDL oxidation, and mild blood pressure reduction, partially offsetting the saturated fat burden. Individuals with established CVD should limit portion size and frequency of consumption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Dual effect: anti-inflammatory cocoa flavanols vs. pro-inflammatory postprandial glucose burden from high GL
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Champorado presents a mixed profile for rheumatoid arthritis: cocoa flavanols (~290mg/bowl) inhibit NF-κB and COX-2 inflammatory pathways and tablea magnesium supports anti-inflammatory enzyme activity, but the very high glycemic load (~GL 60) may generate postprandial advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote systemic inflammation; net clinical effect is uncertain and moderation is advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Champorado is a traditional Filipino sweet chocolate rice porridge made by cooking glutinous (sticky) rice to a thick congee consistency and stirring in tablea — Philippine-style ground cacao discs made from roasted, stone-ground cacao nibs without Dutch-processing, preserving high flavanol content (~580mg flavanols/100g tablea). The porridge is sweetened and enriched with condensed milk and evaporated milk, producing a rich, creamy, deeply chocolatey dish with a thick, sticky texture. A standard 300g bowl provides approximately 504 kcal, 9.6g protein, 13.5g fat, and 87g carbohydrate. Sodium is low at ~135mg per bowl without the traditional tuyo (salted dried fish) garnish. The glycemic index is approximately 69 (medium) but the glycemic load per bowl is very high at ~60, making it a high-risk food for Type 2 Diabetes management. Tablea cacao contributes approximately 290mg cocoa flavanols per bowl, offering cardioprotective and anti-inflammatory benefits. Champorado traces its origin to the Mexican champurrado (a thick cacao-corn masa drink) introduced to the Philippines via the Manila Galleon Trade (1565–1815); the Filipino adaptation substituted glutinous rice for corn masa. The iconic sweet-salty pairing with tuyo is a culturally distinctive Filipino flavour contrast. Champorado is consumed as a breakfast dish, merienda (afternoon snack), and comfort food throughout the Philippines and in Filipino communities in Singapore (notably at Lucky Plaza). It is vegetarian, halal, and gluten-free in its standard preparation without tuyo.
common_user_queries: Filipino chocolate porridge GI glycemic index
Filipino chocolate rice porridge recipe nutrition

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived from FAO/INFOODS SEA Philippines food composition data (approved primary regional source) supplemented with USDA SR Legacy for tablea/cacao and condensed milk components. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for glutinous rice with documented fat-moderation rationale. All core macros are complete and internally consistent. Confidence 1.0 on macros per input provenance.
notes: trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA — no industrial trans fats in traditional champorado ingredients). monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1.0: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for the composite champorado dish (glutinous rice + tablea + condensed milk) is not available as a single entry in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024; component-level MUFA/PUFA data exists but composite dish values are not published. water_g set to -1.0: moisture content for the composite cooked champorado dish is not reported as a single value in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. GI of ~69 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for glutinous rice (GI ~87–98) moderated by fat content from tablea and condensed milk; no direct champorado GI measurement exists in the published literature. GL per 300g serving = (69 × 87g) / 100 = ~60 (very high). Sodium value of ~135mg per 300g bowl is without tuyo garnish; adding tuyo raises sodium to approximately 935mg per bowl. Tablea cacao flavanol content (~580mg/100g tablea) is superior to Dutch-process cocoa due to absence of alkalisation. This record covers the standard preparation without tuyo. Filipino cuisine does not require Chinese, Malay, Tamil, or Hindi local names under Project Veritas multilingual rules (those rules apply to Singaporean dishes only); local_names fields for those languages are appropriately null.
